item.title: Genre Coherence
item.description: Genre coherence is the demand that a nation’s cast of folk types remain stable. Once a nation imagines itself as a collection of authentic genre figures — the Noble Indian, the Hearty Bavarian, the Mountain Kurd, the salt-of-the-earth farmer — the integrity of the national story depends on the characters remaining as assigned. Anyone who does not fit the genre becomes a problem for the story. Not necessarily an ideological enemy, but a narrative disruption: a character who breaks the fourth wall.

item.title: Genring
item.description: Genring is the reduction of a living people to a fixed literary type. It is not a metaphor for stereotyping but a specific political operation with three steps: selection (extracting visually striking, emotionally resonant features from a people’s life — dress, diet, habitat, ritual), codification (assembling those features into a stable character template and circulating it through novels, performances, images, and festivals until it becomes self-reinforcing), and substitution (the template replaces the people, so that encountering the people means encountering the character).

item.title: Temporal Middle
item.description:
The temporal middle is the position of inheriting, continuing, and transforming a tradition without either renewing it wholesale or betraying it. Every living person occupies this position. No one is purely “traditional” (frozen in the past) or purely “modern” (severed from the past). People inherit what they inherit, transform what they can, and continue.

The concept names what settler colonialism, genring, and binary nationalist frameworks systematically eliminate. The elimination operates by offering only two positions: the traditional subject (who matches the assigned genre — the Noble Indian, the Hearty Bavarian, the Mountain Kurd) and the assimilated subject (who has abandoned the genre and joined the dominant culture). There is no position for someone who is doing what every living culture does: inheriting, engaging, adapting, continuing.

item.title: Genre calibration
item.description: A person loses their job during a recession. In the self-help genre, this is a “pivot” — an opportunity to reinvent yourself. In the activist genre, it is evidence of systemic exploitation. In the therapeutic genre, it is a wound requiring healing. The recession is the same recession. The job loss is the same job loss. What changes is the genre — the set of expectations determining what the event means, what feelings are appropriate, and what counts as resolution.

item.title: Californication
item.description: Californication is the process by which liberal governance sustains itself under structural contradiction by formatting crisis as personally manageable affect. The term names what happens when a political system cannot resolve its own contradictions but can teach its subjects to experience those contradictions as personal conditions – as mood, trauma, growth opportunity, or lifestyle. The subject who meditates through a housing crisis, journals through a pandemic, or microdoses through ecological collapse is not failing to respond politically. They are doing exactly what the system requires: converting structural incoherence into self-regulatory behavior.

item.title: Fascist grammar
item.description:
In July 1932, Joseph Goebbels told a crowd of Germans that their nation was collapsing, that the Weimar system was in ruins, that parasites and traitors had corrupted the community, that years of humiliation would be redeemed through struggle, that the German people’s destiny was assured, and that the storm would come and sweep away the old order.

In September 2025, Stephen Miller stood before tens of thousands in Arizona and declared that enemies had tried to kill Charlie Kirk and made him immortal, that “we are the ones who build” while the enemies “create nothing,” that the movement’s lineage stretched from Athens to Rome to Monticello, that “the light will defeat the dark,” and that “we are the storm.”

item.title: Harm governance
item.description: Someone shares a small act of direct action online — stealing banner materials, organizing unpermitted aid, refusing to comply with public health abandonment. The immediate response is not about the target of the action or its consequences. It is about the terms under which it was shared. Was it safe to post? Will someone misinterpret it? Did the actor properly disclose the risk? The pushback is not strategic critique from within an organizing context. It is supervision — the internalized logic of an administrative state, reproduced between people who no longer trust the state itself.

item.title: Industrial intellectualism
item.description: A scholar writes about an abolitionist movement. The movement failed — the facility it opposed was built and is operating. But the paper succeeds. It moves through the correct arc: death-world (thesis), life-worlding (antithesis), building-and-fighting (synthesis). The citations confirm genre membership. The analysis produces the satisfaction the genre demands. The paper circulates, gets cited, gets taught. The facility continues operating. The paper does not need the facility to have been stopped. It needs the narrative to resolve correctly.

item.title: Neurotic platformal intellectual
item.description: Someone posts a detailed analysis of monetary policy on a social media platform. Within hours, people with no economics training are debating the analysis in replies — not asking questions but offering corrections, alternative theories, and confident disagreements. No one does this with surgery. When a surgeon posts about a procedure, non-surgeons do not show up to debate incision technique. But policy analysis on a platform is discourse, and discourse is everyone’s medium. The platform makes no structural distinction between someone who has spent years studying monetary systems and someone who read a thread about inflation that morning. What it registers is engagement.

item.title: Savior-slave subject
item.description: A person sorts their recycling, votes in every election, stays informed about geopolitics, practices mindfulness, donates to mutual aid funds, checks their privilege, buys local, and advocates for systemic change on social media. They are exhausted. They are told — and they believe — that this exhaustion is what responsibility feels like. The systems producing the crises they manage are not accessible to any of these actions. They know this. They continue.

item.title: Zen fascism
item.description: In 1979, Jello Biafra of the Dead Kennedys wrote “California Uber Alles,” a song that identified something no political theorist had yet named: a mode of governance in which serenity is compulsory. The target was Jerry Brown’s California — a political culture that had fused counterculture spirituality with administrative power, producing a regime where the well-governed subject was the calm subject. Not compliant through fear, but compliant through inner peace. The song’s title made the connection explicit: this was authoritarianism, but it smelled like yoga.

item.title: Coherent confusion
item.description:
After the April 2025 tariff announcement, commentators produced thousands of explanations: it was a negotiating tactic, a policy error, a deliberate shock, a market test, a distraction. These explanations contradicted each other, yet each felt plausible. People remained confused — but they kept interpreting, kept producing takes, kept engaging. The system did not need them to understand. It needed them to keep trying.

Coherent confusion names this governance condition: subjects confused but functional, unable to form a stable interpretation of events yet still participating in the systems that produce the confusion. The confusion is not a failure of governance. The confusion is governance. The interpretive labor of trying to make sense of contradictory signals — the takes, the threads, the analyses, the commentary — is itself what sustains system coherence.

item.title: Socially ontogenic media
item.description: A surgeon in a war zone cracks a joke while amputating a leg. The audience laughs — not because it is funny, but because M*A*S*H has spent seasons teaching them that laughter is how you survive a system that processes bodies without pause. The show does not explain how to cope with war. It does not argue a position on war. It models a way of being inside war’s contradictions: ironic, compassionate, rhythmically attuned to crisis that never resolves. The viewer does not learn a lesson. They internalize a posture.
